name: NumPy
summary: Fundamental package for scientific computing in Python, providing powerful N-dimensional array objects.

sections.name: Creation

functions.purpose: Create an array from a list or tuple
Return a new array of given shape filled with zeros
Return a new array of given shape filled with ones
Return evenly spaced numbers over a specified interval
Return evenly spaced values within a given interval

gotchas: Arrays have fixed types; implicit casting can happen silently.
np.empty() allocates uninitialized memory that may contain garbage values; never assume zeros without explicit initialization.

sections.name: Broadcasting and Reshaping

functions.purpose: Change the shape of an array without changing its data
functions.performance_note: np.reshape returns a view when possible, but modifying the view may unexpectedly modify the original array; use .copy() if you need an independent array.

functions.purpose: Return a contiguous flattened array
Permute the dimensions of an array
Expand the shape of an array by inserting a new axis
Remove singleton dimensions from the shape of an array

gotchas: Broadcasting silently expands dimensions by replicating values; adding a shape-(3,) array to a shape-(3,1) array produces a (3,3) result that may not match the intended element-wise operation.
np.reshape() returns a view when possible, but modifying the result may unexpectedly modify the original array; use .copy() if you need an independent array.

sections.name: Indexing and Slicing

functions.purpose: Access elements by index, slice, or boolean mask
Return elements chosen from x or y depending on condition
common_errors: When x and y are scalars and condition is a multi-dimensional array, np.where broadcasts the scalars to match the condition shape, which can produce unexpected results if you intended element-wise selection from arrays of the same shape.

functions.purpose: Take elements from an array along an axis
Select elements where the boolean mask is True
common_errors: Boolean indexing on a 2D array returns a 1-D flattened result regardless of the original shape, which can break downstream code that expects the original dimensionality.

functions.purpose: Select elements using an array of integer indices

gotchas: Fancy indexing with integer arrays returns a copy, not a view; assignments like arr[[0,1]] = 5 work on a temporary copy and may not modify the original array unless you use np.put or np.take with mode='put'.
Boolean indexing creates a 1-D flattened result regardless of input dimensionality; if you need to preserve shape, use np.where() or np.where with a fill value instead.

sections.name: Math and Statistics

functions.purpose: Sum of array elements over a given axis
Compute the arithmetic mean along the specified axis
Compute the standard deviation along the specified axis
Dot product of two arrays
functions.performance_note: For matrix multiplication on ND arrays, use the @ operator or np.matmul instead of np.dot for clearer semantics and better performance with broadcasting.

functions.purpose: Matrix or vector norm
Return indices of the maximum values along the given axis

gotchas: np.mean() on integer arrays performs integer division in NumPy versions before 2.0, silently truncating the fractional part; cast to float first with arr.astype(float) or use np.mean(arr, dtype=float).
np.argmax() returns the first occurrence of the maximum; for arrays with all identical values, it always returns index 0, which can bias downstream sampling or selection logic.

sections.name: Linear Algebra

functions.purpose: Compute the inverse of a matrix
common_errors: Passing a singular or nearly singular matrix raises LinAlgError with a generic message about singular matrices, even when the issue is numerical instability due to high condition numbers.
functions.performance_note: For solving linear systems Ax = b, use np.linalg.solve(a, b) instead of computing the inverse explicitly — it avoids the O(n^3) inverse computation and is numerically more stable.

functions.purpose: Compute the eigenvalues and right eigenvectors of a square matrix
common_errors: For real symmetric matrices, np.linalg.eig returns complex eigenvalues with negligible imaginary parts due to numerical precision, which can break downstream real-only pipelines expecting float arrays.

functions.purpose: Singular Value Decomposition
common_errors: With full_matrices=True (default), the returned U and Vh matrices can be extremely large for wide or tall input matrices, causing memory exhaustion on datasets that fit in RAM otherwise.
functions.performance_note: For dimensionality reduction or low-rank approximations, use full_matrices=False to avoid computing the full orthogonal matrices, reducing memory from O(mn) to O(min(m,n)k) where k is the rank.

functions.purpose: Solve a linear matrix equation

gotchas: np.linalg.inv() is numerically unstable for nearly singular matrices with condition numbers above 1e12; use np.linalg.solve() directly or check np.linalg.cond() before inversion.

np.linalg.eig() returns complex eigenvalues for real matrices when the matrix is not symmetric; np.linalg.eigvals() on a non-symmetric matrix may produce unexpected complex conjugate pairs that break downstream real-only pipelines.
